passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Psychology & Human Behavior
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: How Memory Works
Passage 2: What Motivates People?
Passage 3: Stress — Causes, Effects, and How to Manage It
Passage 4: How We Make Decisions
Passage 5: Personality — What Makes Us Who We Are?
Passage 6: Social Influence — Why We Follow Others
Passage 7: Happiness — What Makes People Feel Good?
Passage 8: Child Development — How Babies Learn About the World
Passage 9: Body Language — Communicating Without Words
Passage 10: Habits — How We Form Them and How to Change Them

Passage 1: How Memory Works
Topic: Memory & the Brain  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Memory is the ability to store and recall information and experiences. It is one of the most important functions of the brain. Without memory, we could not learn new things, recognise people we know, or even remember what we did five minutes ago. Scientists who study the brain have found that memory is not stored in one single place. Instead, different types of memory are handled by different parts of the brain. There are also different types of memory. Short-term memory holds a small amount of information for a short time — for example, remembering a phone number long enough to dial it. Long-term memory stores information for much longer periods — sometimes for a whole lifetime. The process of moving information from short-term to long-term memory is called consolidation.
Paragraph B
Not everything we experience goes into long-term memory. The brain has to decide what is important enough to keep. Several things make it more likely that a memory will be stored for a long time. Repetition is one of the most powerful tools — the more times we encounter or practise something, the stronger the memory becomes. Emotion also plays a big role. Events that are connected to strong feelings — such as excitement, fear, or happiness — tend to be remembered much more clearly than ordinary events. This is why people can often remember exactly where they were when they heard very important news, even many years later. Making connections between new information and things we already know also helps the brain store new memories more effectively.
Paragraph C
Memory is not as reliable as many people think. We often believe that we remember events exactly as they happened, but in reality, memories can change over time. Each time we remember something, the brain does not simply "play back" a recording. Instead, it reconstructs the memory — and this process can be influenced by our feelings, later experiences, and even things other people have told us. This means that two people who witnessed the same event can have very different memories of it. Psychologists call this "reconstructive memory." Because of this, eyewitness accounts in court cases — where a person describes what they saw during a crime — can sometimes be unreliable, even when the witness is completely honest.
Paragraph D
Understanding how memory works has helped people find better ways to learn and study. One of the most effective study methods is called "spaced repetition." Instead of studying everything at once — sometimes called "cramming" — spaced repetition involves reviewing information at regular intervals over a longer period of time. Research shows that this method leads to much better long-term memory than cramming. Another useful technique is self-testing — trying to recall information from memory rather than simply reading it again. When you try to remember something and succeed, the memory becomes stronger. Taking short breaks during study sessions also helps the brain consolidate information. These simple strategies, based on how memory actually works, can make studying much more effective.

Passage 2: What Motivates People?
Topic: Motivation & Behaviour  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Motivation is the force that drives people to act. It is what makes us get out of bed in the morning, work hard at our jobs, and keep trying when things are difficult. Psychologists have been studying motivation for many years, trying to understand what it is that makes people work, learn, and achieve. One of the most well-known ideas about motivation was developed by American psychologist Abraham Maslow in the 1940s. Maslow suggested that human needs can be arranged in a kind of pyramid or "hierarchy." At the bottom of the pyramid are basic needs such as food, water, and shelter. Once these are met, people are motivated by safety, then by the need to belong and feel loved, then by self-esteem, and finally by the desire for personal growth and achieving their full potential.
Paragraph B
Psychologists also make a distinction between two types of motivation: intrinsic and extrinsic. Intrinsic motivation comes from inside the person — you do something because you enjoy it, find it interesting, or feel it is meaningful. A person who reads books because they love reading, or practises music because they find it satisfying, is intrinsically motivated. Extrinsic motivation comes from outside the person — you do something in order to get a reward, such as money, grades, or praise, or to avoid a punishment. A student who studies hard only to get good marks — rather than because they are interested in the subject — is extrinsically motivated. Research shows that intrinsic motivation tends to lead to better learning, more creativity, and greater long-term satisfaction than extrinsic motivation alone.
Paragraph C
One interesting finding from psychology research is that giving people external rewards for something they already enjoy can actually reduce their motivation to do it. This is called the "overjustification effect." In one famous experiment, children who already enjoyed drawing were given a reward for drawing. After the rewards were removed, those children drew less than children who had never been rewarded at all. The explanation is that when we are rewarded for something we like doing, we begin to think of it as "work" rather than something we choose to do freely. This has important lessons for parents and teachers — using prizes and rewards too much can sometimes kill a child's natural love of learning.
Paragraph D
Another important idea in motivation is "goal setting." Research has consistently shown that people perform better when they have clear goals to work towards. Goals give us direction and help us focus our energy. However, not all goals are equally effective. Goals that are specific and slightly challenging — but not so difficult that they seem impossible — tend to produce the best results. Very easy goals do not push people to improve, while goals that seem too hard can cause people to give up. Breaking a big goal into smaller, manageable steps is a useful strategy, as completing each small step gives a feeling of progress and keeps motivation high. Sharing your goals with others — a friend, a colleague, or a coach — can also increase your commitment to achieving them.

Passage 3: Stress — Causes, Effects, and How to Manage It
Topic: Stress & Wellbeing  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Stress is the feeling of being under too much pressure — emotional or physical. Almost everyone experiences stress at some point in their lives. Common causes of stress include problems at work or school, money worries, relationship difficulties, health problems, and major life changes such as moving house or losing a job. Some stress is actually normal and even useful. When we feel a little stressed, our body produces hormones — chemicals — that make us more alert and give us extra energy. This is sometimes called the "fight or flight" response. In small amounts, stress can improve our performance and help us meet deadlines or deal with challenges. However, when stress becomes too intense or lasts for a long time, it can seriously damage our health.
Paragraph B
Long-term stress — often called "chronic stress" — has many negative effects on the body and mind. Physically, chronic stress can cause headaches, muscle tension, stomach problems, sleep difficulties, and a weakened immune system — meaning we get ill more easily. Over time, high levels of stress are linked to serious conditions such as heart disease and high blood pressure. Mentally, chronic stress can cause anxiety, depression, and difficulty concentrating. It can also affect our behaviour — people under a lot of stress may eat too much or too little, drink more alcohol, stop exercising, and withdraw from friends and family. All of these effects make the stress worse, creating a difficult cycle that is hard to break.
Paragraph C
Different people react to the same situation in very different ways. What feels overwhelming to one person may feel manageable or even exciting to another. This is because stress depends not just on the situation itself, but on how we think about it. Psychologists call this "cognitive appraisal." If we see a challenge as something we can handle — a problem with a solution — we are less likely to feel stressed. If we see it as something threatening that we cannot control, we are more likely to feel overwhelmed. This means that the way we think about difficulties has a big impact on how much stress we feel. Learning to change the way we think about stressful situations is one of the key ideas behind Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, or CBT.
Paragraph D
There are many effective ways to manage stress. Regular physical exercise is one of the best — it releases chemicals in the brain that improve mood and reduce the physical effects of stress. Getting enough sleep is also essential, as lack of sleep makes stress much worse. Talking to someone you trust — a friend, family member, or professional counsellor — can help you feel less alone and give you a different perspective on your problems. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, and yoga have been shown to reduce stress hormones in the body. It is also important to organise your time well and set realistic goals, so that you do not feel overwhelmed by too many tasks at once. Finally, limiting the use of alcohol and caffeine, which can both increase feelings of anxiety, is also helpful.

Passage 4: How We Make Decisions
Topic: Decision Making  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Every day, people make hundreds of decisions — from small ones like what to eat for breakfast, to large and important ones like choosing a job or a place to live. For a long time, economists and scientists assumed that people make decisions rationally — that is, by carefully thinking through all the options and choosing the one that will bring them the most benefit. However, research in psychology has shown that human decision-making is often much less rational than we think. Our choices are influenced by emotions, habits, mental shortcuts, and other factors that we are often not even aware of. Understanding how we really make decisions — and the mistakes we often make — can help us make better choices in our lives.
Paragraph B
Psychologists use the word "bias" to describe the mental errors and shortcuts that affect our thinking. One very common bias is called "confirmation bias." This means that people tend to look for and pay attention to information that confirms what they already believe, while ignoring information that goes against their views. For example, someone who believes that a particular diet is healthy will focus on studies that support this view and dismiss studies that suggest it is not. Another common bias is the "anchoring effect" — when people rely too heavily on the first piece of information they receive. If you are told that a bag costs 200 dollars and then given a 20% discount, you feel like you are getting a bargain — even if the bag was never really worth 200 dollars to begin with.
Paragraph C
Emotions play a very powerful role in decision-making. When we feel happy, we tend to make more optimistic choices. When we feel angry or anxious, we often make faster, more impulsive decisions. Fear of loss is one of the strongest emotional influences on our choices. Research has shown that people feel the pain of losing something much more strongly than they feel the pleasure of gaining the same thing. This is called "loss aversion." For example, most people would refuse a coin-flip bet where they could win 100 dollars or lose 100 dollars — because the fear of losing 100 dollars feels stronger than the appeal of winning the same amount. Because of loss aversion, people sometimes stick with a bad situation simply because they do not want to give up what they already have.
Paragraph D
Knowing about the ways our thinking can go wrong can help us make better decisions. One useful strategy is to slow down and think carefully before making an important choice, rather than going with the first idea that comes to mind. Listing the advantages and disadvantages of different options on paper can help us see the situation more clearly. Asking a trusted friend or colleague for their opinion can also be helpful, as they may see things we have missed. It is also useful to ask yourself: "Would I be happy with this choice in five years' time?" This forces you to think about the long-term consequences rather than just the immediate feelings. Being aware of common biases — like confirmation bias and loss aversion — can help us catch ourselves making these errors before we act on them.

Passage 5: Personality — What Makes Us Who We Are?
Topic: Personality & Individual Differences  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Personality refers to the patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving that make each person unique and that stay fairly consistent over time. Even from a very young age, children show different personalities. Some are naturally shy and quiet, while others are outgoing and talkative. Some are calm and easy-going, while others tend to worry or get upset easily. Personality is interesting to study because it helps explain why people behave differently in the same situations and why some people find certain things — such as social situations, new experiences, or difficult challenges — easier or harder than others. Psychologists have developed several systems for describing and measuring personality.
Paragraph B
One of the most widely used models of personality in psychology is called the "Big Five." This model suggests that personality can be described using five main dimensions. The first is Openness — how curious, creative, and open to new experiences a person is. The second is Conscientiousness — how organised, careful, and reliable a person is. The third is Extraversion — how sociable, outgoing, and energetic a person is. People who score low on extraversion are called introverts — they prefer quieter environments and tend to recharge their energy by spending time alone. The fourth dimension is Agreeableness — how kind, cooperative, and trusting a person is. The fifth is Neuroticism — how easily a person experiences negative emotions like anxiety, worry, and sadness.
Paragraph C
A key question in personality research is: where does personality come from? Are we born with our personality, or is it shaped by our experiences and environment? The answer seems to be both. Studies of identical twins — who share 100% of their genes — show that twins raised apart often have surprisingly similar personalities, which suggests that genes play an important role. At the same time, our personality is also shaped by our upbringing, our culture, our relationships, and the experiences we have throughout our lives. Psychologists generally agree that personality is the result of a combination of genetic and environmental factors. While some parts of our personality are quite stable throughout life, other aspects can change — particularly during major life events or as we grow older.
Paragraph D
Understanding your own personality can be very useful in everyday life. For example, knowing that you are an introvert may help you understand why large social events feel tiring, and why you need time alone to recover. Knowing that you score high on conscientiousness may explain why you find it hard to relax when tasks are unfinished. In the workplace, understanding personality differences can help teams work together more effectively. Managers can assign tasks that match each team member's natural strengths. However, it is important not to use personality as an excuse for bad behaviour, or to think that personality cannot change. Research shows that people can develop and change aspects of their personality with effort and the right experiences — personality is not fixed for life.

Passage 6: Social Influence — Why We Follow Others
Topic: Social Psychology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Humans are social animals. We live in groups, and what other people think and do has a big influence on our own behaviour. This is the subject of social psychology — the study of how people are affected by others around them. One of the most basic ways that other people influence us is through a process called "conformity." Conformity means changing your behaviour or opinions to match those of the people around you. It often happens without us even realising it. For example, if you are in a cinema and everyone starts laughing, you are likely to start laughing too — even if you did not think the joke was that funny. Or if everyone at a dinner table is eating slowly and talking quietly, you are likely to do the same.
Paragraph B
A famous experiment on conformity was carried out by American psychologist Solomon Asch in the 1950s. In his experiment, Asch showed people a line on a card and asked them to say which of three other lines was the same length. The answer was always obvious — but other people in the room (who were secretly working with the researcher) gave the wrong answer. Asch found that about 75% of participants gave the wrong answer at least once, simply to avoid being different from the group — even though they could clearly see the correct answer. This showed how powerful the pressure to conform can be, even in simple and clear situations. However, Asch also found that if even one other person gave the correct answer, participants were much more likely to do so too.
Paragraph C
Another type of social influence is obedience — following instructions from someone in authority. Psychologist Stanley Milgram carried out a famous and controversial experiment in the 1960s. Participants were told by a researcher in a white coat to give electric shocks to another person whenever they made a mistake in a test. (In reality, the shocks were not real — the "victim" was an actor.) Milgram found that about 65% of participants continued to give what they believed were very painful shocks, simply because a person in authority told them to. This experiment shocked the world because it showed that ordinary people could do harmful things under pressure from authority, even when they felt uncomfortable about it.
Paragraph D
Understanding social influence is important because it helps explain many real-world behaviours — from following fashion trends and buying products that everyone else has, to more serious situations such as peer pressure among teenagers or people joining harmful groups. Knowing about social influence can help us think more independently and make our own choices. One useful strategy is to pause before going along with what everyone else is doing and ask yourself: "Is this really what I want? Or am I doing this just because others are?" It also helps to find people who share different views and to actively seek out information that challenges our own opinions. This can help us avoid simply following the crowd and make more thoughtful, independent decisions.

Passage 7: Happiness — What Makes People Feel Good?
Topic: Positive Psychology & Wellbeing  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Happiness is something that almost everyone wants, but it can be surprisingly difficult to understand or achieve. In recent decades, psychologists have studied happiness more seriously than ever before, trying to find out what really makes people feel satisfied and content with their lives. This area of research is called "positive psychology." Researchers have discovered that many common assumptions about happiness are actually wrong. For example, many people believe that having more money will make them much happier. Research shows that this is only partly true — having enough money to meet basic needs does improve happiness, but beyond a certain level, more money makes very little difference to how happy people feel day to day.
Paragraph B
Research in positive psychology has identified several things that genuinely do make people happier. Strong relationships with family, friends, and colleagues are one of the most powerful sources of happiness. People who have close, supportive relationships tend to be significantly happier and even healthier than those who are lonely or socially isolated. Doing work or activities that feel meaningful and challenging — not too easy and not too hard — also produces a strong sense of satisfaction. Psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi called this feeling "flow" — the state of being completely absorbed in an activity that you find both difficult and rewarding. Regular physical exercise, getting enough sleep, and spending time in nature have also all been shown to improve happiness and wellbeing.
Paragraph C
One of the most surprising findings in happiness research is the importance of gratitude. Studies have found that people who regularly take time to think about and appreciate the good things in their lives — rather than focusing on what they lack — tend to feel happier and more positive. A simple habit recommended by many psychologists is to write down three things you are grateful for each day. This is called a "gratitude journal." Another important finding is that spending money on experiences — such as a holiday, a meal with friends, or attending a concert — tends to make people happier in the long run than spending money on material things like a new phone or a designer bag. This is because experiences become memories that we can enjoy for years, while the excitement from buying things fades quickly.
Paragraph D
It is also important to understand the concept of "hedonic adaptation" — our natural tendency to get used to things and return to a similar level of happiness over time. When something good happens to us — like getting a pay rise or buying a new car — we feel very happy at first. But after a few weeks or months, we tend to get used to it and return to roughly the same level of happiness as before. This helps explain why winning the lottery or buying expensive things does not make people as happy as they expect. The good news is that adapting to positive changes means that bad events also tend to feel less devastating over time. Understanding hedonic adaptation can help us focus our time and money on things that give lasting satisfaction, rather than chasing experiences that quickly lose their shine.

Passage 8: Child Development — How Babies Learn About the World
Topic: Developmental Psychology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Babies are born with an amazing ability to learn. From the moment they are born, they begin to take in information about the world around them through their senses — sight, hearing, touch, taste, and smell. In the first few years of life, the human brain grows faster than at any other time. By the age of three, a child's brain has already formed over a trillion connections between brain cells. These early years are often called a "critical period" for development — a time when the brain is especially ready to learn and when experiences have a particularly strong and lasting effect. Research consistently shows that the quality of care, attention, and stimulation that babies and young children receive in their first years has a huge impact on their future development.
Paragraph B
One of the most important milestones in early childhood is learning to talk. Babies begin to understand language before they can speak. A newborn baby can already recognise its mother's voice from before birth. By around six months, babies start making simple sounds and babbling. Most children say their first words at around twelve months and begin combining two words by around eighteen months to two years. By the age of five, most children can understand and use thousands of words and have learned the basic rules of grammar. Children learn language fastest when adults talk to them often, read to them, and respond to what they say. Too much screen time — watching TV or videos — has been shown to slow language development if it replaces this kind of interaction.
Paragraph C
Swiss psychologist Jean Piaget is one of the most important figures in the history of child development research. Through careful observation of children — including his own — Piaget developed a theory that children go through four main stages of mental development. In the first stage, babies explore the world through their senses and physical actions. In the second stage, young children begin to use language and pretend play, but they can only see the world from their own point of view. In the third stage, children can think logically about concrete objects and situations. In the fourth stage, which begins in adolescence, people become able to think in more abstract and complex ways — about ideas, possibilities, and hypothetical situations. Piaget's ideas changed the way educators think about teaching children of different ages.
Paragraph D
Attachment — the close emotional bond between a baby and its caregivers — is another very important aspect of early development. British psychologist John Bowlby argued that forming a strong, secure attachment with at least one adult is essential for a child's healthy emotional and social development. Children who develop a secure attachment tend to feel confident exploring the world, knowing they have a safe base to return to. They also tend to develop better relationships with others as they grow older. Children who do not form a secure attachment — for example, because their caregivers are absent, inconsistent, or unresponsive — may struggle with trust, emotional regulation, and forming close relationships later in life. Supporting parents and caregivers to provide warm, responsive care is therefore one of the most effective ways to improve children's long-term wellbeing.

Passage 9: Body Language — Communicating Without Words
Topic: Non-verbal Communication  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
When people communicate, they use more than just words. A large part of the message we send to others comes from body language — the movements, gestures, facial expressions, and postures we use without speaking. Some researchers have suggested that body language accounts for as much as 55% of the meaning we communicate in face-to-face conversations, with tone of voice accounting for a further 38%, and the actual words used only 7%. While these exact percentages have been questioned by other experts, it is widely agreed that non-verbal communication plays a very important role in how we understand each other and how others see us.
Paragraph B
Facial expressions are one of the most important and universal types of body language. Research by psychologist Paul Ekman showed that six basic emotions — happiness, sadness, anger, fear, disgust, and surprise — are expressed through very similar facial expressions in people from very different cultures around the world. This suggests that these expressions are at least partly built into us naturally, rather than being learned from our culture. Eye contact is also a very powerful form of non-verbal communication. Making good eye contact signals confidence, interest, and honesty. Avoiding eye contact can suggest shyness, dishonesty, or lack of interest. However, the appropriate amount of eye contact varies between cultures — in some cultures, looking someone directly in the eye for too long is seen as rude or aggressive.
Paragraph C
Our posture and the way we hold our body also sends clear messages to others. Standing or sitting with an open, upright posture — shoulders back, head up — signals confidence and openness. Crossing your arms across your chest is often interpreted as a sign of being defensive or closed, though it may simply mean that the person is cold or comfortable. Leaning towards someone suggests interest and engagement, while leaning away suggests the opposite. Personal space — how far people stand from each other — also varies between cultures and situations. In many Western countries, people prefer more personal space during conversations, while in some other cultures, standing closer together is normal and friendly.
Paragraph D
Understanding body language can be very useful in everyday life and in professional situations. In a job interview, for example, sitting up straight, smiling, and making good eye contact can create a positive impression — even before you have said a word. Being aware of your own body language can help you appear more confident and approachable. Reading other people's body language can also give you useful information — if someone is fidgeting, looking away, or crossing their arms during a conversation, they may be feeling uncomfortable or uninterested. However, it is important not to jump to conclusions based on a single gesture. Body language should always be interpreted in context, and cultural differences must be taken into account. A gesture that means one thing in one country may mean something very different in another.

Passage 10: Habits — How We Form Them and How to Change Them
Topic: Behaviour & Psychology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
A habit is a behaviour that we do regularly and often automatically — without thinking consciously about it. Brushing your teeth in the morning, checking your phone when you wake up, taking the same route to work every day — these are all examples of habits. Habits form because the brain is always looking for ways to save energy. When we repeat a behaviour many times in the same situation, the brain gradually moves it from conscious thought to automatic action. This means we can do it with very little mental effort. Habits make life more efficient — imagine if you had to think carefully about every single movement involved in driving a car. However, habits can also be harmful — for example, eating unhealthy snacks out of boredom, or spending hours on social media without intending to.
Paragraph B
Researchers who study habits have found that most habits follow a three-step pattern called the "habit loop." The first step is a "cue" — a trigger that tells the brain to start a behaviour. This could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or another person. The second step is the "routine" — the behaviour itself. The third step is the "reward" — the feeling of satisfaction or pleasure that follows the behaviour and tells the brain to remember this habit for the future. For example, feeling bored (cue) leads to picking up your phone (routine), which gives a feeling of entertainment (reward). Understanding this loop is very useful because it shows that to change a habit, you do not need to completely eliminate the cue or the reward — you just need to change the routine.
Paragraph C
Breaking a bad habit is difficult, but there are some effective strategies. One important approach is to identify the cue that triggers the bad habit and find a different routine that gives a similar reward. For example, if you always reach for a biscuit when you feel stressed (cue: stress, reward: comfort), you could try doing a short walk or deep breathing instead (different routine, similar reward: stress relief). Another strategy is to change your environment. If unhealthy snacks are not in your house, you are much less likely to eat them. If your phone is in another room, you will check it less. Making bad habits harder to do and good habits easier to do — by changing your physical environment — is one of the most powerful tools for behaviour change.
Paragraph D
Building new, positive habits is also challenging but very achievable. Research suggests that it takes an average of around 66 days of regular repetition for a new behaviour to become automatic — though this varies widely from person to person and depends on the complexity of the habit. Experts advise starting very small. If you want to exercise more, start by doing just two minutes of exercise every day, rather than trying to go to the gym for an hour three times a week. Once the small habit is established, it is much easier to build on it. It also helps to attach a new habit to an existing one — for example, doing your new exercise immediately after brushing your teeth. This is called "habit stacking" and makes it easier for the brain to remember the new behaviour.
